rights.basis: The work was composed and transmitted in the late fourteenth or early fifteenth century and has no modern authorial layer in its underlying text.

entities.description: A composite fifteenth-century English parchment devotional codex in two parts and contemporary binding. Version L occupies fols. 114r-132v according to Schaffner; the current summary catalog groups relevant contents only under the generic item Religious treatises and does not name Life of Soul or its folios.

holding.notes: Life of Soul version L is at fols. 114r-132v according to Schaffner; this locus is not itemized in the current abbreviated Bodleian record.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Laud Misc. 210
notes: The generic catalog work key work_14353 is not treated as an exact authority identifier for Life of Soul.

localCopies.label: Huntington HM 502 complete currently retrievable five-image selection
notes: The official page advertises twenty labeled gallery slots, but fifteen carry null image URLs and Image not found placeholders; all five non-null resources were retained.
The bundle is complete for the five currently retrievable resources but partial for the advertised gallery and for the physical codex.
ZIP contains the normalized source URL list, exact-source inventory, and five verified 1200-pixel JPEG derivatives.

workPortion.label: Life of Soul, version H
locators: fols. 34v-35r, 58v-59r, and 60v-61r; target text is fols. 35r-60v
notes: Three selected openings overlap or bound the target; they do not reproduce the intervening folios or the complete Life of Soul.
Slot 3 shows fols. 34v-35r and the opening of version H; slot 4 shows fols. 58v-59r; slot 5 shows the f. 60v boundary.
The page's React data contains five non-null image URLs (slots 1, 3, 4, 5, and 20) and fifteen null placeholders, so twenty labels must not be reported as twenty downloadable images.

rights.basis: The manuscript's medieval intellectual content is public domain by age, but neither the selected-image page nor the inspected IIIF Image API response assigns an item-specific rights statement to the modern photographs. Huntington's official reproduction policy says it generally does not own the intellectual content or copyright in library holdings, places clearance responsibility on users, authorizes reproductions for private study, scholarship, or research within applicable law, and asks for appropriate credit.
rights.component: The five modern digital photographs in the published HM 502 selection
notes: The research cache preserves public 1200-pixel derivatives and provider identifiers; it does not assert that the photographs themselves are public domain or authorize repository redistribution.

entities.description: A partial digital surrogate represented by the five non-null IIIF Image API resources exposed in the official page's twenty-slot gallery data on 2026-07-15. next_steps: Request written clarification from U-M Library and, if needed, Paul Schaffner addressing the transcription, markup, notes, nonprofit redistribution, commercial redistribution, and the older embedded terms separately.
Ask Kyvero Press whether the CME Unlicense is intended to cover imported source-text files despite the license's software wording.
Until clarified, retain all rights layers and do not select the most permissive statement as automatically controlling.
openQuestions.question: Do U-M and Schaffner consider the restrictive redistribution statement embedded in the pinned 2003-style XML superseded by the current No Copyright-United States label and public-domain dedication, and how does the CME repository Unlicense apply to this item?

next_steps: Ask Schaffner or U-M whether project files retain a reel identifier, vendor, date, provenance, or frame list.
Ask Bodleian Special Collections whether a historical reproduction order or institutional microfilm exists for the shelfmark.
Compare any identified film with the Queen's University Belfast MS 59/48 listing without assuming identity.
openQuestions.question: What exact film or microfilm copy of Laud Misc. 210 did Schaffner use, and did it cover the full codex or only fols. 114r-132v?

next_steps: Submit a shelfmark-specific enquiry to Bodleian Special Collections asking about internal scans, legacy films, reproduction history, and any forthcoming digitization.
If no complete object exists, request a preservation-approved full-codex reproduction and an explicit image inventory.
Re-run exact Digital Bodleian API and catalog searches before changing access status.
openQuestions.question: Is there a complete authorized digital or photographic surrogate of MS. Laud Misc. 210 that is absent from the public Digital Bodleian search index?

next_steps: Inspect complete versions A and H from the manuscripts or Moon edition, recording every vocative and speaker transition.
Determine whether Schaffner transposed version labels, summarized shifting roles, or relied on a variant passage.
Until resolved, describe only the directly attested incipits and avoid assigning fixed pair labels to A or H.
openQuestions.question: How should the interlocutor roles in versions A and H be described when Schaffner's headnote says A is sister/brother and H father/son, but Arundel A opens Dere fader and inspected HM 502 H opens Broþer and later addresses Dere suster?

next_steps: Obtain authorized copies through Fordham, ProQuest, or a research library.
Collate their title matter, base-text statements, apparatus, commentary, witness descriptions, and interlocutor-role analysis.
Do not treat the 1978 publication as byte- or page-identical to the dissertation until compared.
openQuestions.question: What textual and critical revisions distinguish Pimpl's 1963 dissertation from Moon's 1978 publication, and what exact apparatus does each supply for L, A, and H?

reviewNotes: Abstract work, textual versions, physical witnesses, reproduction medium, scholarly editions, electronic encodings, repository artifact, catalog descriptions, and supporting scholarship are distinct entities.
The exact TEXT match between the pinned artifact and 2016 XML does not collapse the serializations because their headers, availability statements, byte counts, and file identities differ.
Schaffner's 1997/1999 transcription is the direct editorial ancestor; Moon's edition was consulted, not used as the transcription base.
The unidentified film through which Schaffner read Laud is represented as a facsimile/reproduction medium with unknown present survival and provenance.
A dated search finding no Digital Bodleian target is evidence of no public object found, not proof that the manuscript has never been photographed or digitized.

Huntington's public record advertises twenty labeled gallery slots, but its page data currently supplies only five non-null image URLs; three overlap or bound Life of Soul, and neither the five-image resource set nor the advertised gallery is a complete codex.

The current U-M No Copyright-United States statement, Schaffner's current public-domain dedication, the pinned XML's restrictive notice, and the CME repository Unlicense are retained as separate rights assertions rather than silently reconciled.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is the pinned CME serialization of University of Michigan item lifesoul, Paul F. Schaffner's version-L transcription of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Laud Misc. 210, fols. 114r-132v. Its TEXT subtree is byte-identical under XPath serialization to Schaffner's live 2016 XML, while the headers differ materially: the pinned artifact retains a restrictive 1999 availability notice and the live file dedicates the transcription and markup to the public domain. The 2016 XML descends through Schaffner's 2003 readable TEI P3 SGML and 1999 TEI P3 SGML layers from his 1997 HTML transcription, which he made from an unidentified film of Laud. Life of Soul is an abstract anonymous work with three separately modeled textual versions, each currently represented by one manuscript: L in Laud Misc. 210, A in British Library Arundel MS 286, and H in Huntington HM 502. Moon's 1978 edition is Laud-based and collates A and H; Schaffner consulted Moon but did not transcribe from it. No complete public Laud facsimile or IIIF manifest was found in the dated Digital Bodleian audit, Arundel has no linked digitised content, and the HM 502 page advertises twenty labeled gallery slots but currently supplies only five retrievable image resources, three of which overlap or bound Life of Soul.

catalogingSubject.label: Life of Soul (version L)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English prose work Life of Soul as represented by version L in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Laud Misc. 210, fols. 114r-132v. The cataloged intellectual unit comprises six question-and-answer exchanges, the last of which occupies most of the tract. It is not the Laud codex as a physical object, versions A or H as interchangeable copies, Moon's three-witness edition, Schaffner's modern translation or abridgment, the U-M encodings, or the checked-in repository XML. Versions A and H remain collateral textual versions in the lineage record rather than content silently merged into version L.

assertion.notes: Schaffner says about 1400 or a little earlier, Macmillan calls the work late fourteenth century, and MED uses a c1400 citation stencil. These formulations are compatible but do not justify a narrow normalized range.

dateStatements.display: Late fourteenth century or about 1400; exact date unresolved
notes: The fifteenth-century dates of the three surviving codices are witness dates, not composition dates.

assertion.notes: The Huntington catalog dates HM 502 between 1400 and 1450. Laud Misc. 210 and Arundel 286 are currently cataloged only to the fifteenth century.

dateStatements.display: First half of the fifteenth century in Huntington Library HM 502; the relative copying order of L, A, and H remains unresolved

scope.description: Earliest narrowly dated surviving witness currently verified, not necessarily the earliest copy

extent.description: One unheaded DIV1 contains 12 alternating SP and SPEAKER elements: six [question] and six [answer] turns. The body has 62 P elements and 38 continuous folio milestones from 114r through 132v. It also has 121 NOTE1, 49 ADD, 30 DEL, 29 SUPPLIED, 17 UNCLEAR, 7 ITEM, 4 SIC, 2 CORR, and 2 GAP elements. These are encoding metrics; the six exchanges are not separately headed divisions, and the final answer contains most of the tract.

formStatements.description: Continuous Middle English prose organized as six alternating questions and answers; the final answer forms most of the tract.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Biblical references in Schaffner's notes are editorial apparatus; they are not a secondary language of the medieval work.
The XML's inherited AUTHOR value names Bodleian MS Laud Misc. 210 and is treated as a physical-source field mistakenly mapped into authorship.

content_structure_status is summary because the six exchanges are structurally recoverable but lack stable internal titles or separately encoded divisions; inventing six content parts would overstate the XML's hierarchy.

Schaffner's headnote supplies a three-version census, but its prose labels for the A/H interlocutor pairs conflict with the primary incipits and inspected HM 502 image; the lineage record preserves this as an unresolved descriptive conflict rather than propagating the role mapping.

next_steps: Consult Moon's full 1963/1978 linguistic commentary and Macmillan's 2020 chapter.
Check whether a current Linguistic Atlas or dialect study distinguishes authorial language from the three scribal versions.
Do not substitute witness production places or dates for composition evidence.
openQuestions.question: Can current linguistic and textual scholarship narrow the composition date and regional localization beyond late fourteenth century or c1400 in the English Midlands?

next_steps: Review Macmillan's 2020 work-specific chapter and later scholarship for direct engagement with oath-taking, Eucharistic language, dominion, and vernacular biblical use.
Keep the present tag qualified and do not classify the anonymous author as a Lollard without stronger evidence.
openQuestions.question: Does current scholarship accept, qualify, or reject Schaffner's suggestion that Life of Soul may reflect Wycliffite sympathies?

notes: This does not assign Oxford, London, San Marino, or Ann Arbor as the place of medieval composition; those are holding or digital-production places.
The physical manuscript named in the inherited XML AUTHOR field is not an intellectual author.
The dialogue speakers are anonymous roles and are not evidence for an author name.
This is a transmission-layer responsibility, not authorship of the medieval work.

scope.description: Version-L transcription, editorial intervention, notes, and electronic presentation
Near-diplomatic transcription of version L from an unidentified film of Laud Misc. 210, fols. 114r-132v

subjects.label: Commandments, deadly sins, virtues, and works of mercy
Biblical quotation and exposition
notes: Schaffner estimates that more than half of the tract consists of biblical quotation; this record does not convert that estimate into a computed percentage.

assertion.notes: The intended audience is not securely recoverable; Schaffner argues that version L appears suited to a lay audience while acknowledging that its counsels also fit religious readers.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English catechetical and devotional prose dialogue on how the soul reaches heaven. Five short preliminary exchanges establish Christ as the life of the soul and ask what nourishes that life; the long sixth answer develops faith in Christ and obedience to the two commandments of love through the creed, Ten Commandments, seven deadly sins, corporal works of mercy, and six virtues. Biblical quotation and allusion carry much of the argument, while repeated emphasis on motive presents good works as the fruit of love rather than an external checklist.

Life of Soul survives in three versions, each currently known from one manuscript: L in Bodleian MS. Laud Misc. 210, fols. 114r-132v; A in British Library Arundel MS 286, fols. 115r-129r; and H in Huntington HM 502, fols. 35r-60v. The repository text is Schaffner's version-L transcription, ultimately made from an unidentified film of Laud, not Moon's edition and not a collation of all three witnesses.

Schaffner identifies features compatible with Wycliffite sympathy, including treatment of oaths, Eucharistic language, dominion, lay piety, and vernacular scripture, but explicitly calls the evidence inconclusive. The record therefore retains a qualified possible-Wycliffite-sympathies research tag and an open question rather than assigning the work or author categorically to Lollardy.

notes: The version qualifier exposes the exact Laud-based boundary. It does not claim that L is a separate medieval work unrelated to A and H.
The spelling appears as the XML header's TYPE=130 title; it is not represented as an authorial rubric in version L.

scope.description: Opening of version L after the editorial [question] speaker label

titles.value: Frend in crist as seynt poule seiþ· We ne hauen here no cyte· þat is dwelling· but we sechen on þat is to come here after·

Sire in þes two comaundementes of loue is al goodnesse fulfild· & al euel eschewed· & so þe lyuynge of oure soule & þe riȝt weye to heuene is to be leuyn stedefastliche in crist & to kepe þes two comaundementes of loue.

work.summary: An anonymous Middle English catechetical and devotional prose dialogue on how the soul reaches heaven. Five short preliminary exchanges establish Christ as the life of the soul and ask what nourishes that life; the long sixth answer develops faith in Christ and obedience to the two commandments of love through the creed, Ten Commandments, seven deadly sins, corporal works of mercy, and six virtues. Biblical quotation and allusion carry much of the argument, while repeated emphasis on motive presents good works as the fruit of love rather than an external checklist.
